skill.name: prompt-engineering-patterns
skill.description: Master advanced prompt engineering techniques to maximize LLM performance, reliability, and controllability in production. Use when optimizing prompts, improving LLM outputs, or designing production prompt systems.
skill.summary: Master advanced prompt engineering techniques including chain-of-thought, few-shot learning, and template systems for reliable AI interactions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 216 static findings are false positives. The flagged files are markdown documentation (.md) and example JSON files containing educational content about prompt engineering techniques. The scanner incorrectly interprets backticks in markdown code blocks as shell commands, text references to cryptographic terms as weak crypto implementations, and tutorial references to system commands as reconnaissance. This is a documentation skill with no executable security issues.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/Shell Backtick Detection in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 170 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in markdown files. These are false positives - the backticks are markdown code block delimiters showing Python/code examples in documentation, not actual shell commands. Files affected: SKILL.md, references/*.md, assets/*.md
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown files contain educational code examples in fenced code blocks. Backticks are documentation formatting, not executable shell commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm References
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 39 instances of 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' in documentation files. These are false positives - the files contain educational content explaining prompt engineering patterns, with text examples mentioning cryptographic concepts in context of AI safety, not actual crypto implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documentation files contain text about security concepts in prompt engineering tutorials, not actual cryptographic code or implementations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System/Network Reconnaissance in Tutorials
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'System reconnaissance' and 'Network reconnaissance' patterns in markdown documentation. These are false positives - the files contain educational tutorials that reference system commands and networking concepts as part of prompt engineering examples, not actual reconnaissance tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Educational documentation about prompt patterns includes references to system commands in tutorial context, not actual security scanning tools.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Filesystem Path Traversal in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Path traversal sequence' in references/prompt-optimization.md and scripts/optimize-prompt.py. The markdown file contains text explaining path handling concepts in prompts. The Python script is a utility for prompt optimization with legitimate file operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documentation references path concepts in tutorial context. The Python script uses standard file I/O for prompt optimization, not user-controlled path traversal.

content.value_statement: Improve LLM outputs with proven prompt engineering techniques. This skill provides patterns for chain-of-thought reasoning, few-shot learning, and template systems that make AI interactions more reliable and controllable.
actual_capabilities: Design chain-of-thought prompts that elicit step-by-step reasoning from LLMs
Implement few-shot learning with dynamic example selection strategies
Create reusable prompt templates with variable interpolation and conditionals
Build system prompts that define AI assistant behavior and constraints
Optimize prompts for consistency, accuracy, and token efficiency
Debug and refine prompts that produce inconsistent outputs
limitations: Does not execute code or interact with external APIs directly
Requires user to provide specific use case context for best results
Pattern effectiveness varies depending on underlying LLM model
Cannot guarantee output correctness for complex reasoning tasks

use_cases.title: Build Reliable AI Products
use_cases.description: Design production-ready prompt systems with consistent output formats and error handling patterns for AI-powered applications.

use_cases.title: Improve Code Generation
use_cases.description: Apply structured prompting techniques to get better code completion and generation results from Claude or Codex.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers using AI coding assistants

use_cases.title: Create AI Training Materials
use_cases.description: Develop comprehensive prompt libraries and templates for team-wide AI adoption and best practices.

prompt_templates.title: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
prompt_templates.prompt:
Solve this problem step by step:

Problem: {problem}

Think through each step carefully:
1. [First step]
2. [Second step]
3. [Third step]

Final answer:
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex reasoning tasks requiring detailed explanation

prompt_templates.title: Few-Shot Classification
prompt_templates.scenario: Text classification with specific category examples

prompt_templates.title: System Prompt Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Defining AI assistant behavior and constraints

prompt_templates.title: Iterative Refinement
prompt_templates.scenario: Improving outputs through multiple refinement rounds

output_examples.input: Design a prompt that helps users write professional emails
output_examples.output: A structured email writing template with role definition, tone guidelines, and format sections that produces consistent, professional email outputs.

output_examples.input: How can I improve code review quality with AI?
output_examples.output: A few-shot prompt providing code review examples with common bug patterns, security considerations, and best practices that guides the AI to provide thorough, constructive feedback.

best_practices: Start with clear role definitions in system prompts to establish AI behavior boundaries
Use specific, diverse examples in few-shot prompts that cover edge cases
Test prompts across multiple query variations to ensure consistent outputs
anti_patterns: Using vague instructions like 'be helpful' without specific behavioral guidelines
Overloading prompts with too many examples that exceed context window limits
Assuming prompts work identically across different LLM models without testing

faq.question: What is chain-of-thought prompting?
faq.answer: Chain-of-thought prompting encourages LLMs to show their reasoning step-by-step, improving accuracy on complex math, logic, and reasoning tasks.

faq.question: How many examples should I include in few-shot learning?
faq.answer: Most tasks work well with 2-5 examples. Balance example diversity with context window limits. Too many examples can reduce response quality.

faq.question: Can I use these patterns with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes, these patterns work with Claude, Claude Code, Codex, and most modern LLMs. Claude Code can execute the prompt templates directly.

faq.question: How do I debug prompts that produce inconsistent outputs?
faq.answer: Add explicit output format constraints, include more diverse examples, and use step-by-step reasoning to make the AI process more transparent.

faq.question: What is the difference between system prompts and user prompts?
faq.answer: System prompts define the AI assistant's role and behavior at the start of conversation. User prompts contain the specific task or question for each interaction.

faq.question: How do I handle prompt injection risks?
faq.answer: Use clear instruction boundaries, validate user inputs, and avoid concatenating untrusted content directly into prompts. Structure prompts with distinct sections for instructions and user data.
